name: construct (Binary Data DSL)

performance.notes: Pure Python parsing; slower than struct for simple cases but far more expressive

agent_readiness.notes: Declarative binary data parsing and building. from construct import *. format = Struct('magic' / Const(b'PNG\r\n\x1a\n'), 'width' / Int32ub, 'height' / Int32ub, 'data' / Bytes(this.width * this.height)). result = format.parse(b'...'). result.width, result.height. format.build(Container(width=100, height=200, data=b'...')). Primitives: Int8ub, Int16ul, Float32b, Bytes(n), String(n, 'utf8'). Computed: Computed(this.width * 2). Arrays: Array(this.count, Int16ul). Switch: Switch(this.type, {0: Int8ub, 1: Int16ub}). Enum: Enum(Byte, a=1, b=2). Bitwise: BitStruct. LazyBound for recursive. Tunnels: Compressed. GreedyRange.

gotchas: construct 2.x and 2.9+ have breaking API changes — check version carefully
Python lambda/this context can be confusing — this.field refers to sibling fields
Slower than struct module for simple structures — don't use for hot paths
No type annotations — Container is a dict-like object without typing
Error messages for parsing failures can be cryptic with nested structures
best_when: Parsing binary file formats (PNG, ELF, PE, network packets, firmware)
Protocol implementation where both parse() and build() are needed
Reverse engineering binary formats with complex conditionals
Security research: network protocol implementation, firmware analysis
avoid_when: Simple fixed-width binary structs (use Python struct module instead)
Performance-critical hot paths — overhead is non-trivial
When Kaitai Struct is preferable (multi-language compiled parsers)
summary: construct is a Python DSL for declarative binary parsing and building. Struct('width' / Int32ub, 'height' / Int32ub). format.parse(bytes) and format.build(Container()). Symmetric parse/build. this.field for cross-references. Arrays, Switches, Enums, Bitfields. MIT by Tomer Filiba. Best for binary format parsing and protocol implementation. 2.x breaking API changes. Slower than struct for simple cases. No type annotations. Excellent for security research and file format RE.
